description: Deterministic money math rules — ITS Stage 4: Money Math. Rules validate that the student's pricing, costs, and revenue goals are financially viable.

rules.name: Price below cost
rules.message: If your selling price is lower than what it costs to make each unit, every sale makes you poorer — fix this before anything else.
rules.socratic_hint: The student's unit price is at or below their unit cost. Ask them to list every cost that goes into one unit — materials, packaging, time, fees. Then ask: what price do you need to charge to make at least $1 per sale?

rules.name: Break-even beyond archetype-appropriate threshold
rules.message: ⏰ It would take too long to earn back your startup money for this type of business. Try raising your price, increasing your monthly sales target, or cutting startup costs.
rules.socratic_hint: The student's break-even is beyond the typical range for their business type. Ask: what's one startup cost you could eliminate or delay? Or: what price increase would cut the break-even time in half?

rules.name: Monthly profit too low to be worthwhile
rules.message: 💸 Your monthly profit is very low — after covering costs, you're barely earning anything for your time. Raise your price, reduce your costs, or sell more units.
rules.socratic_hint: The student's monthly profit is very low. Ask: if you divide your monthly profit by your hours worked, what's your hourly rate? Is that worth it to you? What would need to change to make it worthwhile?

rules.name: No reinvestment plan
rules.message: 📈 A business that puts nothing back into itself can't grow — try to save at least 20% of your profit to reinvest (buy more materials, better tools, etc.).
rules.socratic_hint: The student plans to spend all profit. Ask: what would you need to buy more of to scale up — more materials? Better tools? Could reinvesting 20% get you there faster?

rules.name: No contingency buffer
rules.message: 🛡️ Every first business owner underestimates costs! Add a safety buffer of at least 20% extra to your budget — otherwise you might run out of money before you even start.
rules.socratic_hint: The student hasn't budgeted a contingency. Ask: name one thing that could go wrong in the first month — spoiled ingredients, a broken tool, a refund request. How much would that cost? Is that in your budget?

rules.name: Implied hourly rate below minimum wage
rules.message:
⏱️ Your monthly profit divided by your hours worked comes to less than $5/hour — below most minimum wages. Is your time worth that little?

What to do: Try raising your price, reducing your cost per unit, or selling more units. Even a small price increase can dramatically change your hourly rate.
rules.socratic_hint: The student's implied hourly rate is below $5. Walk them through the calculation: monthly profit ÷ (hours/week × 4 weeks) = hourly rate. Then ask: would you do any paid job for that rate? What is the smallest price increase that would bring your hourly rate above $10?

rules.name: Zero or missing startup cost
rules.message: 💡 You've entered £0 for startup costs — almost every business has at least some initial expense (materials, packaging, a domain name, printing, tools). Double-check: is there anything you need to buy or pay for before your first sale?
rules.socratic_hint: The student entered zero startup cost. Ask: what is the very first thing you would need to spend money on before making your first sale — even if it's just a bag, a poster, or an app subscription? List three things and estimate a cost for each.
